sanskrit_devanagari:
सञ्जय उवाच ।
तं तथा कृपयाविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम् ।
विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sañjaya uvāca |
taṁ tathā kṛpayāviṣṭam aśru-pūrṇākulekṣaṇam |
viṣīdantam idaṁ vākyam uvāca madhusūdanaḥ || 1 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; body text byte-identical to canonical; vedabase renders without internal line-breaks but the akṣara sequence matches)
exact (text body identical; line-breaks present; double-danda renders as ||)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs — single danda vs double danda)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.001 = MBh_06,024.001). GRETIL writes 'aśru-pūrṇa' as 'aśrupūrṇa' (no hyphen); body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: Madhusūdana (Krishna, slayer of the demon Madhu)

translations.verbatim_quote: Sañjaya said: Seeing Arjuna full of compassion, his mind depressed, his eyes full of tears, Madhusūdana, Kṛṣṇa, spoke the following words.
Sanjay said: Seeing Arjun overwhelmed with pity, his mind grief-stricken, and his eyes full of tears, Shree Krishna spoke the following words.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Material compassion, lamentation and tears are all signs of ignorance of the real self. Compassion for the eternal soul is self-realization. The word "Madhusūdana" is significant in this verse. Lord Kṛṣṇa killed the demon Madhu, and now Arjuna wanted Him to kill the demon of misunderstanding that had overtaken him in the discharge of his duty.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.copyright_holder: Advaita Ashrama, Kolkata (publisher of Swami Gambhirananda's English translation of Sankara-bhasya). No verbatim is captured because no commentary exists for this verse.
commentaries.notes: Site bhagavad-gita.us states explicitly that Sri Sankaracharya did not comment on this sloka and that his commentary starts from 2.10. Standard Advaita Ashrama (Gambhirananda) edition agrees - Sankara's bhasya begins at BG 2.10.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.1 – 2.3 Sanjaya said — Lord said — When Arjuna thus sat, the Lord, opposing his action, said: ‘What is the reason for your misplaced grief? Arise for battle, abandoning this grief, which has arisen in a critical situation, which can come only in men of wrong understanding, which is an obstacle for

positions.rendering: kṛpā here is 'material compassion' — a sign of ignorance of the real self, not a virtue

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators render kṛpayā as compassion or pity, but the Gaudiya Vaishnava purport reads it pejoratively (sentimental compassion for the body, not the soul) while modern devotional translations leave it neutral. This matters: the chapter's diagnosis hinges on whether Arjuna's pity is misdirected or merely overwhelming.

literal_meaning: Sanjaya said: To him (Arjuna), thus overwhelmed by pity, his eyes full of tears and agitated, despondent, Madhusūdana (Krishna) spoke these words.
traditional_meaning_consensus: A purely narrative verse: Sanjaya, narrating to the blind king Dhritarashtra, sets the scene at the opening of Chapter 2. Arjuna remains in the collapsed state from the end of Chapter 1 — overwhelmed, weeping, sunk in lamentation. Krishna, named Madhusūdana, is about to begin the teaching that occupies the rest of the Gita. The verse carries no doctrinal claim of its own; its function is to mark the transition from Arjuna's collapse to Krishna's teaching response. The naming of Krishna as 'Madhusūdana' (slayer of Madhu) is the only doctrinally suggestive element — read by some traditions as foreshadowing the slaying of internal confusion that the chapter is about to perform.

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim — site states he did not comment on 2.1; bhasya begins at 2.10.
